assumptions: The question concerns consolidated financial statements.
The dividend-related balance is a recognized intragroup receivable and therefore a monetary item.
The receivable is exposed to foreign currency risk because the relevant group entities do not share the same functional currency.
No fact provided indicates that the balance is part of a net investment in a foreign operation.

approaches.label: Foreign currency hedge of an intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: In consolidation, intragroup items are generally excluded, but IFRS 9 expressly allows the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item such as a receivable/payable if the FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
conditions: The balance is an intragroup monetary item
The FX exposure gives rise to gains or losses not fully eliminated on consolidation
The relevant group entities have different functional currencies
Formal designation and hedge documentation exist at inception
The hedge meets IFRS 9 effectiveness requirements
approaches.practical_implication: A compliant hedge designation is possible if the entity documents the receivable's FX risk and demonstrates the consolidation exception and hedge effectiveness.

source_contributions.summary: Only items with parties external to the reporting entity are normally eligible as hedged items in consolidation (6.3.5).
FX risk of an intragroup monetary item may qualify in consolidated financial statements if resulting FX gains/losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation (6.3.6).
The hedge must be formally designated and documented at inception and satisfy hedge effectiveness requirements; risk components must be identifiable and measurable (6.3.7, 6.4.1, B6.3.8).

approaches.label: Forecast intragroup transaction hedge
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 has a separate exception for highly probable forecast intragroup transactions, but the fact pattern describes an already recognized dividend receivable rather than a forecast transaction.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not rely on the forecast-transaction exception for an already recognized intragroup dividend receivable.

source_contributions.summary: A highly probable forecast intragroup transaction may qualify only if it is foreign-currency denominated and the FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss (6.3.6, B6.3.5).
The transaction must be highly probable and the hedging relationship must meet IFRS 9 documentation and effectiveness requirements (6.3.3, 6.4.1).

approaches.label: Net investment hedge in a foreign operation
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 applies only to hedges of foreign currency risk arising from a net investment in a foreign operation and should not be applied by analogy to other hedge relationships such as an ordinary intragroup dividend receivable.
approaches.practical_implication: This model is unavailable unless the exposure is truly part of a net investment in a foreign operation.

source_contributions.summary: The Interpretation applies only to hedges of net investments in foreign operations and not by analogy to other hedge accounting relationships (7-8).

The hedged risk is the FX exposure between the foreign operation's functional currency and a parent's functional currency; the hedge can qualify only once in consolidated financial statements (10-14).
Any such hedge would still need formal designation, documentation and effectiveness under IFRS 9 (6.4.1).
